179	\section{Lifting a short exact sequence to injective resolutions}
180	
181	The passage from the complex-level homology long exact sequence
182	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) to a long exact sequence of
183	right-derived functors requires one geometric input: a short exact sequence of
184	objects must be lifted to a short exact sequence of their injective resolutions
185	which is, moreover, \emph{degreewise split}. This is the dual Horseshoe Lemma. It is the single genuinely new homological-algebra construction the argument
186	requires; individual injective resolutions (provided by enough injectives) furnish
187	the building blocks, and the horseshoe construction assembles them into the required
188	lift.
189	
190	\subsection{The horseshoe construction, step by step}
191	
192	The construction proceeds by induction on degree; we isolate its independent moving
193	parts as the sub-lemmas below, from which the horseshoe lemma is assembled. Throughout,
194	\(I_A\) and \(I_C\) are fixed injective resolutions of \(A\) and \(C\) (they exist
195	because \(\mathcal{A}\) has enough injectives), and we write
196	\(d_A, d_C\) for their differentials and \(I_A^n, I_C^n\) for their degree-\(n\) terms.
197	The middle resolution is built degreewise as the biproduct
198	\(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\). All seven statements are structural pieces of the dual
199	Horseshoe Lemma (dual of Weibel, \emph{An Introduction to Homological Algebra}, Lemma
200	2.2.8); no new source is introduced.
201	
202	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: a finite biproduct of injectives is injective]
203	  \label{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
204	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Injective.instBiprod}
205	  \mathlibok
206	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
207	  In an abelian category, if \(P\) and \(Q\) are injective objects then their biproduct
208	  \(P \oplus Q\) is injective. Consequently each degree term
209	  \(I_B^n := I_A^n \oplus I_C^n\) of the middle complex is injective, being the
210	  biproduct of the injective terms \(I_A^n\) and \(I_C^n\) of the two chosen
211	  resolutions.
212	\end{lemma}
213	
214	\begin{lemma}[Degree term: the biproduct short complex is split exact]
215	  \label{lem:horseshoe_degree_split}
216	  \lean{CategoryTheory.ShortComplex.Splitting.ofHasBinaryBiproduct}
217	  \mathlibok
218	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
219	  For objects \(P, Q\) of an abelian category the short complex
220	  \[
221	    P \xrightarrow{\ \iota_P\ } P \oplus Q \xrightarrow{\ \pi_Q\ } Q
222	  \]
223	  formed from the biproduct coprojection \(\iota_P\) and projection \(\pi_Q\) carries a
224	  canonical \texttt{ShortComplex.Splitting}; in particular it is a split short exact
225	  sequence. Applied in each degree \(n\) with \(P = I_A^n\), \(Q = I_C^n\), this exhibits
226	  every degree
227	  \[
228	    0 \longrightarrow I_A^n \longrightarrow I_B^n \longrightarrow I_C^n \longrightarrow 0
229	  \]
230	  as split, furnishing the degreewise-splitting data required for the construction.
231	\end{lemma}

233	\begin{lemma}\leanok
234	[Per-stage monomorphism into the biproduct]
235	  \label{lem:horseshoe_stage_mono}
236	  \lean{CategoryTheory.mono_biprod_lift_factorThru_of_exact}
237	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
238	  Let \(0 \to S.X_1 \xrightarrow{S.f} S.X_2 \xrightarrow{S.g} S.X_3 \to 0\) be an exact
239	  short complex with \(S.f\) a monomorphism, and let \(\alpha : S.X_1 \to P\),
240	  \(\gamma : S.X_3 \to Q\) be monomorphisms with \(P\) injective. Then the map
241	  \[
242	    \bigl\langle\, \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)\,,\; S.g \circ \gamma \,\bigr\rangle
243	      \;:\; S.X_2 \longrightarrow P \oplus Q,
244	  \]
245	  whose first component is the injective extension of \(\alpha\) along the mono \(S.f\)
246	  and whose second component is \(S.X_2 \twoheadrightarrow S.X_3 \hookrightarrow Q\), is a
247	  monomorphism. This is the kernel/cokernel step driving each stage of the recursion:
248	  applied to the \(n\)-th cosyzygy short exact sequence with \(P = I_A^{n+1}\),
249	  \(Q = I_C^{n+1}\) it produces the monomorphism of the next cosyzygy into the next
250	  biproduct term, whose cokernel feeds the following stage (and, at the base stage with
251	  the original sequence \(0 \to A \to B \to C \to 0\), the augmentation
252	  \(\beta : B \hookrightarrow I_B^0\)).
253	\end{lemma}
254	
255	\begin{proof}
256	  \uses{lem:horseshoe_biprod_injective}
257	  \leanok
258	  Write \(u := \langle \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f), S.g \circ \gamma\rangle\). To see
259	  \(u\) is mono it suffices, in an abelian category, to show \(x \circ u = 0 \implies x = 0\)
260	  for any \(x\). Composing \(x \circ u\) with the two biproduct projections gives
261	  \(x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\) and \(x \circ S.g \circ \gamma = 0\);
262	  since \(\gamma\) is mono the latter yields \(x \circ S.g = 0\). Exactness of \(S\) at
263	  \(S.X_2\) (with \(S.f\) mono) lets us lift \(x\) through \(S.f\): there is \(y\) with
264	  \(y \circ S.f = x\). Then \(y \circ \alpha = y \circ S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f)
265	  = x \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = 0\), using the defining property
266	  \(S.f \circ \mathrm{factorThru}(\alpha, S.f) = \alpha\); as \(\alpha\) is mono, \(y = 0\),
267	  hence \(x = y \circ S.f = 0\).
268	\end{proof}

321	  the dual of the inductive lift in the Horseshoe Lemma.

515	\section{The dimension-shift step}
516	
517	The induction driving the comparison theorem rests on a single short-exact-sequence
518	calculation: cutting a resolution into short exact sequences and reading off the
519	long exact sequence of right-derived functors. The latter is not available off the
520	shelf at the level of the derived-functor objects \((R^k G)\); we build it from the
521	complex-level homology long exact sequence
522	(Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) via the horseshoe lift
523	(Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}). We first record the shift at the
524	\emph{resolution} level — where the horseshoe lift directly supplies the
525	degreewise-split short exact sequence of injective resolutions — and then specialize
526	to objects.
527	
528	\begin{lemma}\leanok
529	[Resolution-level dimension shift]

532	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
533	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
534	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
535	  \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\) be a short exact sequence with \(J\) right-\(G\)-acyclic,
536	  lifted to a \emph{degreewise-split} short exact sequence of injective resolutions
537	  \(0 \to I_A \to I_J \to I_Z \to 0\) (chain maps \(\varphi, \psi\) with
538	  \(\psi \circ \varphi = 0\) and a splitting of \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\)
539	  in each degree \(n\)). Then for every \(k \geq 0\) the connecting map of the homology
540	  long exact sequence of \(G(I_\bullet)\) is an isomorphism
541	  \[
542	    (R^{k+1} G)(Z) \;\cong\; (R^{k+2} G)(A).
543	  \]
544	\end{lemma}
545	
546	\begin{proof}
547	  \uses{def:right_acyclic, lem:right_derived_injective_resolution,
548	    lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}
549	    \leanok
550	  Applying \(G\) degreewise to the degreewise-split sequence preserves each split
551	  degree, yielding a short exact sequence of cochain complexes
552	  \(0 \to G(I_A) \to G(I_J) \to G(I_Z) \to 0\) in \(\mathcal{B}\). Its homology long
553	  exact sequence (Lemma~\ref{lem:homology_long_exact_sequence}) has connecting maps
554	  \(\delta^m : H^m(G(I_Z)) \to H^{m+1}(G(I_A))\); transport these across the
555	  injective-resolution computation \(H^m(G(I_-)) \cong (R^m G)(-)\)
556	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:right_derived_injective_resolution}). For \(m = k+1 \geq 1\) the two
557	  homology objects flanking \(\delta^m\) in the long exact sequence both sit on the
558	  middle complex \(G(I_J)\), namely \(H^{k+1}(G(I_J))\) and \(H^{k+2}(G(I_J))\); both
559	  vanish because \(J\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic (Definition~\ref{def:right_acyclic}).
560	  With both neighbours zero, \(\delta^{k+1}\) is an isomorphism, giving
561	  \((R^{k+1} G)(Z) \cong (R^{k+2} G)(A)\).
562	\end{proof}

615	\begin{proof}

620	  We first construct the long exact sequence of right-derived functors associated
621	  to \(0 \to A \to J \to Z \to 0\). In the classical treatment this sequence is
622	  supplied by the \(\delta\)-functor structure of \(\{R^k G\}\) on the derived
623	  category; here it is built directly from the complex-level homology long exact
624	  sequence via a horseshoe lift.

626	  \emph{Building the long exact sequence.} By the horseshoe lift
627	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:injective_resolution_of_ses}) choose injective resolutions
628	  \(I_A, I_J, I_Z\) of \(A, J, Z\) fitting into a short exact sequence of cochain
629	  complexes

632	  \]

634	  \(0 \to I_A^n \to I_J^n \to I_Z^n \to 0\) is a \emph{split} short exact sequence

636	  functor \(G\) carries it to a split short exact sequence
637	  \(0 \to G(I_A^n) \to G(I_J^n) \to G(I_Z^n) \to 0\) --- this is where degreewise
638	  splitness is essential, since \(G\) is not assumed exact and would not in general
639	  preserve a non-split short exact sequence. Hence

650	      \to H^{k+1}\bigl(G(I_J)\bigr) \to \cdots .
651	  \]

658	  \[

661	  \]

672	  \[

680	  recorded separately in Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_one_iso_coker}.)
681	\end{proof}

752	\section{Cosyzygies of an acyclic resolution}
753	
754	The comparison theorem decomposes an acyclic resolution into the short exact
755	sequences cut out by its cosyzygies, and reads the cohomology of the applied complex
756	off those sequences using left-exactness of \(G\). We isolate the two ingredients
757	here.

759	\begin{lemma}\leanok
760	[Cosyzygy short exact sequences of an acyclic resolution]
761	  \label{lem:cosyzygy_ses}

767	  % SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Say $I^n = 0$ for $n < n_0$. Setting $J^n = \Im(d^n)$ we break
768	  % $I^\bullet$ into short exact sequences

770	  % for $n \geq n_0$."

772	  (\texttt{proposition-enough-acyclics}).}
773	  Let \(G : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}\) be additive and let
774	  \[
775	    0 \longrightarrow A
776	      \longrightarrow J^0 \longrightarrow J^1 \longrightarrow J^2
777	      \longrightarrow \cdots
778	  \]
779	  be an exact resolution of \(A\) with every \(J^n\) right-\(G\)-acyclic. Define the
780	  cosyzygies \(Z^0 := A\) and, for \(m \geq 1\),
781	  \[
782	    Z^m \;:=\; \ker\bigl(J^m \to J^{m+1}\bigr)
783	      \;=\; \operatorname{im}\bigl(J^{m-1} \to J^m\bigr).
784	  \]
785	  Then for every \(m \geq 0\) the sequence
786	  \[
787	    0 \longrightarrow Z^m \longrightarrow J^m \longrightarrow Z^{m+1}
788	      \longrightarrow 0 \tag{$S_m$}
789	  \]
790	  is short exact, with the inclusion \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) the canonical one,
791	  the surjection \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) the corestriction of
792	  \(J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image, and the middle term \(J^m\) right-\(G\)-acyclic.

800	  definition a subobject of \(J^m\) (for \(m = 0\) via the injection \(A \hookrightarrow
801	  J^0\) of the resolution, for \(m \geq 1\) as the kernel \(\ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\)).
802	  The map \(J^m \to Z^{m+1}\) is the corestriction of the differential
803	  \(d^m : J^m \to J^{m+1}\) onto its image \(Z^{m+1} = \operatorname{im}(d^m)\), hence
804	  an epimorphism. Exactness in the middle is exactness of the resolution at \(J^m\):
805	  the image of \(Z^m \hookrightarrow J^m\) equals \(\ker(d^m)\) --- for \(m \geq 1\)
806	  by the definition \(Z^m = \ker(J^m \to J^{m+1})\), and for \(m = 0\) because
807	  exactness of \(0 \to A \to J^0 \to J^1\) makes \(A \hookrightarrow J^0\) identify
808	  \(A\) with \(\ker(d^0)\) --- while the kernel of the epimorphism
809	  \(J^m \twoheadrightarrow Z^{m+1}\) is exactly \(\ker(d^m)\). Thus
810	  \(\operatorname{im}(Z^m \to J^m) = \ker(J^m \to Z^{m+1})\), so \((S_m)\) is short
811	  exact. The middle term \(J^m\) is right-\(G\)-acyclic by hypothesis. The final

9

12	Chapter~\ref{chap:Cohomology_CechHigherDirectImage} compares against: the comparison

14	relative {\v C}ech complex with \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) wherever the derived functor
15	is defined.

18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a
19	quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.

1373	  injective \(\mathcal{I}\) has \(H^n(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) for \(n > 0\). The
1374	  section-level surjectivity \(\mathcal{I}(U) = H^0(U, \mathcal{I}) \to H^0(U,
1375	  \mathcal{Q}) = \mathcal{Q}(U)\) established above makes the connecting map into
1376	  \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F})\) zero, whence \(H^1(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Applying the
1377	  same conclusion to \(\mathcal{Q}\) (another module with vanishing higher {\v C}ech
1378	  cohomology for \(\mathrm{Cov}\)) gives \(H^1(U, \mathcal{Q}) = 0\), and then the
1379	  long exact sequence with \(H^1(U, \mathcal{I}) = H^2(U, \mathcal{I}) = 0\) forces
1380	  \(H^2(U, \mathcal{F}) = 0\). Iterating the dimension shift — at each stage
1381	  \(\mathcal{Q}\) plays the role of \(\mathcal{F}\) — gives \(H^p(U, \mathcal{F}) =
1382	  0\) for all \(p > 0\) and every \(U \in \mathcal{B}\).
1383	
1384	  The argument never uses affine sheaf-cohomology vanishing
1385	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_serre_vanishing}); its only {\v C}ech-vanishing input is
1386	  condition (3), which on the affine instantiation is supplied by the standard-cover
1387	  {\v C}ech vanishing of Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_acyclic_affine}. Thus the dependency
1388	  \(\text{affine Serre vanishing} \to \text{this lemma}\) is one-directional and the
1389	  graph is acyclic.
1390	\end{proof}

1503	  cohomology sheaf of the complex \(f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet\); since the abelian
1504	  structure on \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules computes cokernels and kernels objectwise on
1505	  the level of presheaves and then sheafifies, that cohomology sheaf is the
1506	  sheafification of the presheaf \(V \mapsto H^k\bigl((f_*\mathcal{I}^\bullet)(V)\bigr)

1510	  of a sheafification vanishes exactly when the underlying presheaf vanishes on a basis,

1715	  \texttt{lemma-quasi-coherence-higher-direct-images-application}.}
1716	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a separated quasi-compact morphism of schemes, let
1717	  \(\mathcal{F}\) be a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and let
1718	  \(\mathfrak{U} : X = \bigcup_{i \in I} U_i\) be a finite affine open cover of
1719	  \(X\) (so, by separatedness, every intersection \(U_{i_0 \ldots i_p}\) is
1720	  affine). Then the cohomology sheaves of the relative {\v C}ech complex
1721	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\) of
1722	  Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex} compute the higher direct images: for every
1723	  \(i \geq 0\) there is a canonical isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-modules
1724	  \[
1725	    H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr)
1726	      \;\cong\; R^i f_*\mathcal{F}.
1727	  \]
1728	  In particular, over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), taking global
1729	  sections gives \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) =
1730	  H^0(S, R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\) as \(A\)-modules for all \(i\).
1731	
1732	  Two points about the precise form of the comparison should be noted. First, the
1733	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) on the right-hand side
1734	  is the one obtained from injective resolutions, which is available only when the
1735	  category of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules has enough injectives; accordingly this
1736	  comparison is asserted under the hypothesis that injective resolutions exist in
1737	  \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\), and it compares the (always-defined) {\v C}ech higher
1738	  direct image \(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}))\)
1739	  against the derived-functor one only in that case. Second, we assert the comparison in the
1740	  weak form of the \emph{existence} of an isomorphism, i.e.\ as
1741	  \(\bigl(H^i(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})) \cong
1742	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr)\) without singling out a chosen natural isomorphism.
1743	  This existence statement is all the downstream consumers (representability of the
1744	  relative Picard functor, local triviality of the comparison) require: they use
1745	  only that the unconditional {\v C}ech \(R^i f_*\) and the derived-functor
1746	  \(R^i f_*\) agree as objects, never a specific identification, so committing to a
1747	  canonical natural transformation would add coherence obligations that play no
1748	  role in the applications.
1749	\end{lemma}
1750	
1751	\begin{proof}
1752	  \uses{lem:cech_augmented_resolution, lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic, lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived, def:cech_complex, def:higher_direct_image}
1753	  Apply the abstract Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} with the
1754	  right-derivable additive functor \(G = f_*\)
1755	  (the pushforward \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X) \to \operatorname{QCoh}(S)\)), the object
1756	  \(A = \mathcal{F}\), and the resolution \(J^\bullet = \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) given by
1757	  the {\v C}ech nerve of Definition~\ref{def:cech_nerve}. The two hypotheses of the
1758	  abstract lemma are exactly the geometric sub-lemmas established above:
1759	  \begin{enumerate}
1760	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_augmented_resolution} the augmented {\v C}ech
1761	      complex \(0 \to \mathcal{F} \to \mathcal{C}^0 \to \mathcal{C}^1 \to \cdots\) is
1762	      exact, i.e.\ \(\mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is a resolution of \(\mathcal{F}\) in
1763	      \(\operatorname{QCoh}(X)\).
1764	    \item By Lemma~\ref{lem:cech_term_pushforward_acyclic} each term
1765	      \(\mathcal{C}^p\) is right \(f_*\)-acyclic:
1766	      \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,k\,(\mathcal{C}^p) = 0\) for \(k \geq 1\).
1767	  \end{enumerate}
1768	  Lemma~\ref{lem:acyclic_resolution_computes_derived} then gives, for every \(i\), an
1769	  isomorphism between the \(i\)-th derived functor evaluated at \(\mathcal{F}\) and
1770	  the \(i\)-th cohomology of the complex obtained by applying \(f_*\) to the
1771	  resolution,
1772	  \[
1773	    (f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})
1774	      \;\cong\;
1775	    H^i\bigl(f_*\,\mathcal{C}^\bullet\bigr).
1776	  \]
1777	  By construction (Definition~\ref{def:cech_complex}) the complex \(f_*\,
1778	  \mathcal{C}^\bullet\) is precisely the relative {\v C}ech complex
1779	  \(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\), so its \(i\)-th
1780	  cohomology is \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})
1781	  \bigr)\); and \((f_*).\mathrm{rightDerived}\,i\,(\mathcal{F})\) is the
1782	  derived-functor higher direct image \(R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) of
1783	  Definition~\ref{def:higher_direct_image}. This yields the isomorphism
1784	  \(H^i\bigl(\check{\mathcal{C}}^\bullet(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F})\bigr) \cong
1785	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F}\) asserted by the lemma; taking it in the existence
1786	  (\(\operatorname{Nonempty}\)) form discards the choice of resolution, as required.
1787	  Over an affine base \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), passing to global sections
1788	  recovers \(H^i(X, \mathcal{F}) = \check{H}^i(\mathfrak{U}, \mathcal{F}) = H^0(S,
1789	  R^i f_*\mathcal{F})\).
1790	\end{proof}
